chemicals.name: Rotenone
chemicals.description: A natural pesticide found in certain plants. Blocks electron transfer from the iron-sulfur clusters of Complex I to ubiquinone (CoQ). Prevents NADH oxidation and proton pumping at Complex I.
chemicals.overview: Natural plant-derived insecticide and pesticide. Shuts down the first step of the electron transport chain, preventing cells from using NADH to generate ATP.
chemicals.carcinogenicity: EPA: 'Not Likely to be Carcinogenic to Humans.' No IARC classification. However, linked to Parkinson's disease risk through mitochondrial damage in dopaminergic neurons.

chemicals.name: Piericidin A
chemicals.description: A potent antibiotic produced by Streptomyces. Structurally similar to ubiquinone, it competes for the CoQ binding site on Complex I, blocking electron flow.
chemicals.overview: Bacterial antibiotic that mimics ubiquinone's shape. Tricks Complex I by fitting into the CoQ docking site, blocking electron handoff to the mobile carrier.

chemicals.name: Amobarbital (Barbiturate)
chemicals.description: Barbiturates like amobarbital inhibit Complex I by blocking electron transfer at the NADH dehydrogenase. Reduces proton pumping and NADH-linked respiration.
chemicals.overview: A sedative/anesthetic drug. At high doses it inhibits Complex I, contributing to respiratory depression and cellular energy failure in overdose.
chemicals.carcinogenicity: IARC Group 2B (Phenobarbital, related compound): Possibly carcinogenic to humans. Barbiturates as a class associated with increased liver tumor risk in animal studies.

chemicals.name: Malonate
chemicals.description: A competitive inhibitor of succinate dehydrogenase (Complex II). Structurally similar to succinate, it binds the active site and blocks FADH2-linked electron entry into the ETC via ubiquinone.
chemicals.overview: A molecule that looks like succinate but can't be processed. It occupies Complex II's active site and blocks the FADH2 pathway into the chain. Classic example of competitive inhibition.

chemicals.name: TTFA (Thenoyltrifluoroacetone)
chemicals.description: Inhibits Complex II by blocking electron transfer from FADH2 to ubiquinone at the CoQ binding site. Prevents succinate-driven respiration.
chemicals.overview: A research chemical that blocks the exit point of Complex II where electrons are handed off to CoQ. Used in labs to study the FADH2 branch of the ETC independently.

chemicals.name: Antimycin A
chemicals.description: Binds the Qi site of the cytochrome bc1 complex (Complex III), blocking electron transfer from ubiquinol to cytochrome c. Halts the Q-cycle and proton pumping at Complex III.
chemicals.overview: A fish poison and antibiotic. Jams Complex III's internal recycling mechanism (Q-cycle), causing electrons to leak onto oxygen and generate toxic free radicals.

chemicals.name: Myxothiazol
chemicals.description: Binds the Qo site of Complex III, preventing ubiquinol oxidation. Unlike Antimycin A, it blocks the first step of the Q-cycle.
chemicals.overview: A bacterial antibiotic that blocks the entry point of Complex III where reduced CoQ delivers its electrons. Stops the chain at the midpoint.

chemicals.name: Cyanide (CN⁻)
chemicals.description: Binds to the Fe³⁺ in cytochrome a3 of Complex IV (cytochrome c oxidase), preventing the final transfer of electrons to oxygen. Completely halts the entire ETC.
chemicals.overview: One of the most rapidly lethal poisons. Locks onto the iron in Complex IV so electrons cannot reach oxygen. The entire chain backs up and ATP production stops within seconds.
chemicals.carcinogenicity: Not classified as carcinogenic by IARC, NTP, or EPA. Cyanide is an acute systemic toxin, not a carcinogen — it kills cells by energy deprivation, not DNA damage.

chemicals.name: Carbon Monoxide (CO)
chemicals.description: Binds to the reduced iron (Fe²⁺) of cytochrome a3 in Complex IV, competing with oxygen. Prevents electron transfer to O2 and blocks the entire chain.
chemicals.overview: A colorless, odorless gas from incomplete combustion. Poisons cells in two ways: binds hemoglobin (blocking oxygen delivery) AND directly blocks Complex IV (blocking oxygen use).
chemicals.carcinogenicity: Not classified as carcinogenic by IARC. CO is an acute asphyxiant, not a carcinogen. However, combustion sources that produce CO often co-emit carcinogens (e.g., benzene, PAHs).

chemicals.name: Hydrogen Sulfide (H₂S)
chemicals.description: Inhibits cytochrome c oxidase (Complex IV) by binding to the copper center (CuB). At low concentrations may act as a signaling molecule; at high concentrations it is toxic.
chemicals.overview: The 'rotten egg' gas. At low levels it is a natural signaling molecule in the body. At high levels it poisons Complex IV similarly to cyanide, shutting down aerobic respiration.
chemicals.carcinogenicity: Not classified as carcinogenic by IARC or NTP. Primarily an acute toxic gas. Some research suggests low-level H₂S may actually have anti-cancer properties through mitochondrial signaling.

chemicals.name: Sodium Azide (NaN₃)
chemicals.description: Azide ions bind to cytochrome a3 in Complex IV, similar to cyanide. Blocks electron flow to oxygen and halts oxidative phosphorylation.
chemicals.overview: A chemical preservative and airbag propellant. Highly toxic if ingested — acts like cyanide by blocking Complex IV. Prevents cells from using oxygen to make ATP.
chemicals.carcinogenicity: NTP: Known mutagen (positive in Ames test and other genotoxicity assays). Not formally classified as carcinogenic by IARC, but mutagenic potential warrants caution.

chemicals.name: Oligomycin
chemicals.description: Blocks the proton channel (Fo subunit) of ATP synthase (Complex V). Protons cannot flow back into the matrix, so ATP synthesis stops. The proton gradient builds up, eventually slowing the entire ETC.
chemicals.overview: A fungal antibiotic that plugs the proton channel in ATP synthase. Protons pile up in the IMS with nowhere to go, creating backpressure that eventually stalls the whole chain.

chemicals.name: 2,4-Dinitrophenol (DNP)
chemicals.description: A protonophore that carries H⁺ across the inner mitochondrial membrane, bypassing ATP synthase. Dissipates the proton gradient as heat. Electron transport accelerates but ATP production drops dramatically.
chemicals.overview: A dangerous weight-loss drug banned in the 1930s. Pokes holes in the proton gradient — the energy meant for ATP is wasted as heat instead, raising body temperature dangerously.
chemicals.carcinogenicity: Not classified by IARC. NTP: Limited evidence. DNP is primarily an acute metabolic poison (hyperthermia), but its nitroaromatic structure raises concern for potential mutagenicity.

chemicals.name: FCCP
chemicals.description: Carbonyl cyanide-p-trifluoromethoxyphenylhydrazone. A potent protonophore that shuttles H⁺ across the membrane. Used to measure maximal respiratory capacity in experiments.
chemicals.overview: A powerful research uncoupler. Carries protons across the membrane like a shuttle bus, completely bypassing ATP synthase. Used to measure how fast the ETC can run without any brakes.

chemicals.name: CCCP
chemicals.description: Carbonyl cyanide m-chlorophenylhydrazone. Similar to FCCP, it dissipates the proton gradient by shuttling protons across the inner membrane independently of ATP synthase.
chemicals.overview: Another research protonophore, functionally identical to FCCP. Collapses the proton gradient so the ETC runs freely but makes no ATP — all energy is released as heat.

chemicals.name: Thermogenin (UCP1)
chemicals.description: Uncoupling Protein 1, naturally expressed in brown adipose tissue. Creates a regulated proton leak across the inner membrane, generating heat instead of ATP. This is non-shivering thermogenesis.
chemicals.overview: The body's natural heater. Found in brown fat, it deliberately wastes the proton gradient as heat to keep newborns and hibernating animals warm without shivering.

chemicals.name: Atractyloside
chemicals.description: Inhibits the adenine nucleotide translocase (ANT), which normally exchanges ADP (into matrix) for ATP (out of matrix). Blocks ATP export from mitochondria, causing intramitochondrial ATP accumulation and feedback inhibition.
chemicals.overview: A plant toxin from thistles. Blocks the transporter that ships ATP out of the mitochondria. ATP gets trapped inside while the rest of the cell starves for energy.

chemicals.name: Metformin
chemicals.description: First-line diabetes drug that mildly inhibits Complex I. Reduces ATP production, activating AMPK which increases glucose uptake and reduces hepatic glucose output. Effect is much weaker than rotenone.
chemicals.overview: The world's most prescribed diabetes medication. Gently slows Complex I just enough to trigger the cell's energy sensor (AMPK), which boosts glucose uptake — a therapeutic side effect of mild ETC inhibition.
chemicals.carcinogenicity: Not carcinogenic. Epidemiological studies consistently show metformin is associated with REDUCED cancer risk. Actively studied as a potential anti-cancer agent due to AMPK activation and metabolic effects on tumors.

chemicals.name: MPP⁺ (from MPTP)
chemicals.description: MPTP is converted to MPP⁺ by MAO-B in the brain. MPP⁺ is selectively taken up by dopaminergic neurons and inhibits Complex I, causing energy depletion and oxidative stress.
chemicals.overview: A synthetic contaminant discovered in tainted street drugs. Converted to MPP⁺ in the brain, where it selectively kills dopamine neurons by poisoning their Complex I — causing rapid-onset Parkinson's disease.

chemicals.name: Doxorubicin (Adriamycin)
chemicals.description: An anthracycline chemotherapy drug that can accept electrons from Complex I and transfer them directly to O2, generating superoxide radicals. This redox cycling damages mitochondrial components.
chemicals.overview: A widely used chemotherapy drug. It hijacks electrons from Complex I and dumps them onto oxygen, creating toxic free radicals that kill cancer cells — but also damage the heart's mitochondria-rich cells.
chemicals.carcinogenicity: IARC Group 2A: Probably carcinogenic to humans. Paradoxically, this anti-cancer drug can itself cause secondary cancers (especially leukemia) due to DNA damage from the same free radicals that kill tumor cells.
